data.content:
Execution environment baseline (Docker worker):
- OS/runtime: Debian-based container with shell access.
- Core CLI: bash, sh, git, gh, copilot, curl, wget, tar, unzip, zip, make.
- Data/query tooling: jq, yq, sqlite3.
- Search/inspection: ripgrep (rg), fd, tree, less, findutils, coreutils, moreutils, file, xxd.
- Sync/automation: rsync, fzf, entr.
- Container tooling: docker CLI + dockerd (DinD capable with privileged runtime).
- Process/network diagnostics: procps (ps), lsof, netcat (nc), dnsutils (dig), iputils-ping (ping), tcpdump, traceroute, mtr, strace, ltrace (amd64 builds).
- Document/shell tooling: pandoc, shellcheck, shfmt, tini.
- Language runtimes/toolchains: python3 + pip3 + venv, node + npm, openjdk-17 (java/javac), ruby, php, go + gofmt, rustc + cargo, gcc + g++, cmake, pkg-config.
- Go developer tooling: gopls, goimports, dlv, staticcheck, golangci-lint, gotestsum, mockgen, govulncheck, task.

Use only tools available in this environment. Prefer deterministic, non-interactive commands, and keep file writes scoped to explicit project paths provided by the task.

Taskboard synthesis contract:

- You are a taskboard synthesis agent.

- Build a canonical execution taskboard from the provided project documents.

- Unless the user explicitly narrows scope, treat the taskboard as the complete project scope for this ingestion run.

- When multiple repositories exist, break work down clearly by repository and ensure every task is explicitly attributable to a single target repository.

- Extract epics and actionable tasks, preserve dependencies, avoid inventing unsupported facts, and output a deterministic plan suitable for execution orchestration.

Ingestion execution instructions:

- Generate a full-featured taskboard JSON from the decoded project documents.

- Unless the user explicitly asks for partial scope, treat the generated taskboard as the complete scope for this ingestion run.

- Decompose work into the smallest practical execution chunks where each epic and each task represents one concrete outcome.

- Use explicit dependencies where supported by the source materials; treat depends_on as a required modeling tool, not an optional hint.

- Whenever one task could block, invalidate, or conflict with another task, add an explicit depends_on relationship to serialize execution.

- Keep dependencies acyclic and reference only existing IDs.

- Put first-executable tasks early in each epic.tasks list; preserve topological ordering whenever possible.

- Keep independent work parallel by avoiding unnecessary dependencies.

- Do not create duplicate or near-duplicate tasks (including renamed variants with the same intent).

- Do not create parallel sibling tasks that could implement the same behavior in different files/packages.

- If two candidate tasks touch the same artifact, behavior, or acceptance outcome, merge them into one canonical task OR link them with explicit depends_on.

- Prefer one implementation-owner task per behavior change; model additional checks as dependency-aware verification tasks.

- Separate implementation from verification; verification tasks should validate one check per task and avoid bundled checks.

- Ensure task titles are specific and disambiguated by concrete scope (artifact/package + action) so duplicate intent is obvious and prevented.

- Do not invent unsupported requirements or implementation facts.

- Synchronized source repositories (if present) are available under: repos/<repository-name>

- Use the selected source branch for each repository when resolving source context.

- Repository layout contract: local source cache is projects/{projectId}/repositories/{repository-name}, then copied into sandbox ./repos/{repository-name}.

- For multi-repository projects, decompose work by repository and ensure every task is clearly mapped to exactly one target repository.

- Include board metadata with repository scope and source branch: metadata.repositories[] and metadata.source_branch.

- For every epic, include repository_ids as a non-empty string array of project repository IDs impacted by that epic.

- For every task, include repository_ids as a non-empty string array of project repository IDs impacted by that task.

- For every epic and task, include deliverables as a non-empty string array of concrete outputs.

- Keep repository_ids and deliverables explicit and deterministic; do not leave them blank.

- Output must be plain-text JSON only (no markdown, no prose, no code fences).

- Write valid JSON only to this exact output path: /tmp/ingestion-sandbox-2107252471/sandbox/taskboard.json

- The JSON must match the exact schema below; do not add extra fields.

- Exact schema contract:
{
  "board_id": string,
  "run_id": string,
  "name"?: string,
  "state": "pending" | "active" | "completed" | "failed",
  "epics": [
    {
      "id": string,
      "board_id": string,
      "title": string,
      "objective"?: string,
      "repository_ids": string[],
      "deliverables": string[],
      "state": "planned" | "in_progress" | "completed" | "blocked" | "failed",
      "rank": number,
      "depends_on_epic_ids"?: string[],
      "tasks": [
        {
          "id": string,
          "board_id": string,
          "epic_id": string,
          "title": string,
          "description"?: string,
          "repository_ids": string[],
          "deliverables": string[],
          "task_type": string,
          "state": "planned" | "in_progress" | "completed" | "failed" | "no_work_needed",
          "rank": number,
          "depends_on_task_ids"?: string[],
          "audits"?: [
            {
              "model_provider": string,
              "model_name": string,
              "model_version"?: string,
              "model_run_id"?: string,
              "agent_session_id"?: string,
              "agent_stream_id"?: string,
              "prompt_fingerprint"?: string,
              "input_tokens"?: number,
              "output_tokens"?: number,
              "started_at"?: RFC3339 timestamp,
              "completed_at"?: RFC3339 timestamp
            }
          ],
          "outcome"?: {
            "status": "success" | "partial" | "failed",
            "summary": string,
            "error_code"?: string,
            "error_message"?: string
          }
        }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "ingestion_audits"?: [
    {
      "model_provider": string,
      "model_name": string,
      "model_version"?: string,
      "model_run_id"?: string,
      "agent_session_id"?: string,
      "agent_stream_id"?: string,
      "prompt_fingerprint"?: string,
      "input_tokens"?: number,
      "output_tokens"?: number,
      "started_at"?: RFC3339 timestamp,
      "completed_at"?: RFC3339 timestamp
    }
  ],
  "created_at": RFC3339 timestamp,
  "updated_at": RFC3339 timestamp
}

- Ensure board_id is "v1_documentation_1772679817378162637" and run_id is "ingest-1772679817378162637".

- Ensure all epic/task board_id values match board_id.

- Required board fields: board_id, run_id, state, epics, created_at, updated_at.

- Required epic fields: id, board_id, title, repository_ids, deliverables, state, rank.

- Required task fields: id, board_id, epic_id, title, repository_ids, deliverables, task_type, state, rank.

- Board state values: pending, active, completed, failed.

- Epic state values: planned, in_progress, completed, blocked, failed.

- Task state values: planned, in_progress, completed, failed, no_work_needed.

- For outcomes, include outcome.status and outcome.summary.

Remote document guidance:

- Selected remote documents are optional input.

- If no selected remote documents are provided, infer context from synchronized repositories and explicit user requirements.

No remote documents were selected for this ingestion run.

Synchronized source repositories:

- repository_id=agentic-repo-1 repository_url=https://github.com/shanehughes1990/agentic-worktrees source_branch=v1 local_dir=repos/agentic-worktrees

User requirements:

Audit all existing documents in the `v1` folder, update them to match the current implementation, and deliver a complete V1 project documentation set that covers every component and how it currently works, prioritizing accuracy and full scope coverage.

Execution continuity:
- stream_id: ingestion-agent-1772679817378651512
- session_id: 
- Reuse this same stream/session for retries and continuation.

1. # TODO: Worker Supervisor Scheduler Dependency
2. 
3. ## Discovery Summary
4. 
5. The worker runtime still depends on `taskengine.Scheduler` for supervisor-driven enqueue flows, even after splitting Asynq into API/Worker platforms.
6. 
7. ### Where this dependency exists
8. 
9. - `internal/core/worker/worker.go`
10.   - Bootstraps scheduler via `taskengine.NewScheduler(...)`
11.   - Wires `SetAdmissionLedger(...)` and `SetAdmissionSignalSink(...)`
12.   - Passes scheduler into `applicationcontrolplane.NewService(...)`
13.   - Builds supervisor dispatcher with scheduler
14. 
15. - `internal/infrastructure/supervisor/taskengine/dispatcher.go`
16.   - Uses scheduler to enqueue follow-up jobs from supervisor decisions (`scheduler.Enqueue(...)`)
17. 
18. - `internal/application/controlplane/documents.go`
19.   - Uses scheduler for project document task enqueue paths when service is constructed in worker runtime
20. 
21. ## Why this matters
22. 
23. Goal is to keep worker as a pure Asynq consumer/processor. Current scheduler usage means worker can still originate enqueue operations in some flows.
24. 
25. ## Follow-up tasks
26. 
27. 1. Define target boundary:
28.    - API is sole enqueue/admission owner
29.    - Worker is consumer-only (register/start/shutdown handlers)
30. 
31. 2. Remove worker-side enqueue origins:
32.    - Refactor supervisor dispatcher usage so enqueue decisions are executed by API-side orchestration
33.    - Prevent worker-constructed control-plane service from owning enqueue paths
34. 
35. 3. Update worker bootstrap:
36.    - Remove scheduler creation from worker runtime once enqueue paths are removed
37.    - Keep only `WorkerPlatform` consumer lifecycle (`Register`, `Start`, `Shutdown`)
38. 
39. 4. Preserve distributed contract:
40.    - Ensure any handoff from worker observations to API enqueue remains via persisted/distributed mechanisms
41. 
42. 5. Verification:
43.    - Add/adjust tests proving worker no longer calls `scheduler.Enqueue(...)`
44.    - Run full `go test ./...`
45. 
46. ---
47. 
48. # TODO: Realtime Worker Registry + Heartbeat Refactor (LISTEN/NOTIFY First)
49. 
50. ## Status
51. 
52. - Current: completed
53. - End state target: completed
54. - Completion gate: do not mark completed until desktop + API + worker compile successfully and full project tests pass again
55. 
56. ## Scope mandate
57. 
58. This is a complete refactor/rewrite of the worker registration + heartbeat subsystem.
59. 
60. - Do not deliver this as a shim, bridge layer, or minimal tactical patch
61. - Do not preserve old polling/legacy lifecycle behavior behind feature toggles
62. - Replace the existing flow with the new realtime-first model as the primary architecture
63. - Remove superseded paths once replacement behavior is verified
64. - Breaking changes are explicitly accepted for this refactor
65. - No migration path is required for previous worker heartbeat/registration flow
66. - No backward-compatibility guarantees are required for legacy contracts in this subsystem
67. 
68. ## End-to-end implementation plan
69. 
70. ### 1) Domain contracts and lifecycle model
71. - [ ] Define durable registration submission domain contracts (request, response, status, reason, timeout, revoke)
72. - [ ] Define realtime heartbeat request/response contracts (API initiated, worker response, deadline semantics)
73. - [ ] Define explicit worker lifecycle transitions for new model (`pending_registration`, `registered`, `healthy`, `invalidated`, `deregistered`)
74. - [ ] Remove/replace any remaining legacy state semantics not used by the new flow
75. 
76. ### 2) Infrastructure persistence (minimal Postgres)
77. - [ ] Add minimal durable registration submission persistence (create/list pending/update status/revoke)
78. - [ ] Keep worker registry persistence minimal and aligned with new lifecycle only
79. - [ ] Remove obsolete persistence fields and write paths that are no longer reused
80. - [ ] Ensure API startup catch-up query path processes pending registration submissions
81. 
82. ### 3) Realtime transport abstraction and PG implementation
83. - [ ] Keep/establish transport abstraction at domain/application boundaries
84. - [ ] Implement/align PostgreSQL LISTEN/NOTIFY adapter for registration + heartbeat channels
85. - [ ] Ensure deterministic envelopes (correlation IDs + idempotency keys)
86. - [ ] Ensure transport usage remains swappable for future non-PG backend
87. 
88. ### 4) API runtime implementation
89. - [ ] API listens for registration submissions in realtime and judges compatibility
90. - [ ] API responds accept/reject in realtime and persists outcome atomically
91. - [ ] API periodically initiates heartbeat requests to registered/healthy workers via realtime transport
92. - [ ] API enforces deadline, invalidates missed workers, and persists deregistration/invalidation
93. - [ ] API startup catch-up handles pending submissions created while API was down
94. 
95. ### 5) Worker runtime implementation
96. - [ ] Worker startup creates durable registration submission + realtime notify
97. - [ ] Worker waits for API decision with bounded timeout/retry policy
98. - [ ] Worker revokes pending submission on timeout before exiting non-zero
99. - [ ] Worker exits non-zero on reject and logs explicit reasons
100. - [ ] Worker handles API heartbeat requests and responds with health proof
101. - [ ] Worker handles API invalidation/shutdown intent and exits non-zero when live
102. - [ ] Worker restart always performs fresh registration; old epoch/session never reused
103. 
104. ### 6) Desktop scope (frontend)
105. - [ ] Update desktop worker-related GraphQL operations/models to new contract shape
106. - [ ] Preserve desktop realtime visibility for registered worker count and worker session information
107. - [ ] Remove desktop dependencies on deleted legacy fields/events
108. - [ ] Ensure desktop builds cleanly with updated schema/generated artifacts
109. 
110. ### 7) GraphQL/schema/codegen reconciliation
111. - [ ] Finalize GraphQL schema for worker sessions/settings/events under new model
112. - [ ] Regenerate GraphQL server artifacts and frontend/client artifacts
113. - [ ] Remove stale generated code and stale resolver paths no longer used
114. 
115. ### 8) Test strategy and required coverage
116. - [ ] Add API tests: registration accept/reject, startup catch-up, heartbeat request loop, invalidation on missed proof
117. - [ ] Add worker tests: timeout revoke path, reject non-zero path, heartbeat response path, shutdown intent non-zero exit path
118. - [ ] Add transport tests: abstraction conformance + PG adapter behavior
119. - [ ] Add persistence tests: submission durability, pending query, status transitions, revoke semantics
120. - [ ] Add desktop tests for worker realtime/session/count rendering against new contract
121. - [ ] Remove obsolete tests from deleted legacy behavior
122. 
123. ### 9) Compile and verification gate
124. - [x] API compile/build green
125. - [x] Worker compile/build green
126. - [x] Desktop compile/build green
127. - [x] Full backend test suite green (`go test ./...`)
128. - [x] Frontend test suite green (desktop/frontend tests)
129. - [ ] End-to-end regression passes for registration + heartbeat lifecycle
130. 
131. ### 10) Completion status update
132. - [x] Update this TODO status from `implementing` to `completed` only after all compile + test gates above pass
133. 
134. ## Target behavior
135. 
136. Workers rely on API-owned runtime configuration persisted in the database, and worker registration records remain persisted in the database. Coordination and liveness signaling move to a realtime-first PostgreSQL `LISTEN/NOTIFY` pipeline.
137. 
138. Registration compatibility requests must be durable: each worker startup request is persisted in the database and also emitted through realtime `NOTIFY` for low-latency handling.
139. 
140. Heartbeat policy is API-initiated and proof-based:
141. - Workers may claim `healthy`, but API is the authority that periodically requests heartbeat proofs from currently `registered`/`healthy` workers
142. - Missing/failed proofs cause API-side force deregistration of that worker epoch
143. - If the worker process is still alive, it must receive shutdown intent and exit with non-zero status
144. - If the worker was offline during invalidation and later restarts, it must submit a new registration; old epoch/registration is invalid
145. 
146. Postgres persistence for this subsystem should be minimal:
147. - Keep authoritative current worker registry state and runtime settings
148. - Keep durable registration submission records needed for startup compatibility handshake/replay
149. - Do not add heartbeat audit history retention tables/logs for this flow
150. 
151. Realtime transport architecture must remain swappable:
152. - Treat realtime heartbeat/registration messaging as an abstraction at domain/application boundaries
153. - Provide a concrete PostgreSQL `LISTEN/NOTIFY` implementation for now
154. - If abstraction and concrete implementation are not both present, add them as part of this refactor
155. - Keep contracts deterministic so transport can be replaced later (for example, a stronger broker) without changing core orchestration logic
156. 
157. ## Refactor requirements
158. 
159. 1. Realtime registration compatibility handshake:
160.    - On worker startup, persist a registration compatibility submission record in DB and publish a corresponding realtime `NOTIFY` event
161.    - API consumes request, validates worker build/runtime compatibility (version + remote backend/runtime config expectations)
162.    - API returns accept/reject response over the same realtime pipeline
163.    - API startup path must scan and process pending registration submissions from DB (for requests created while API was unavailable)
164.    - Worker must not register in DB unless API explicitly responds with `ok`
165.    - Worker waits for response with bounded timeout; on timeout worker revokes/cancels its pending DB submission and exits non-zero
166.    - Worker exits non-zero on explicit reject and logs reject reasons at error level
167. 
168. 2. API-initiated heartbeat proving:
169.    - API is the initiator and periodically requests heartbeats from all workers currently marked `registered`/`healthy`
170.    - Requests and responses flow through `LISTEN/NOTIFY` (no polling-first model)
171.    - Worker responds to API heartbeat request with proof-of-health payload
172.    - On missed response deadline, API force-deregisters the worker epoch as invalid
173.    - API emits shutdown intent/event for live workers so runtime terminates with non-zero exit
174. 
175. 3. DB as source of truth + realtime as transport:
176.    - Keep worker registry/settings in DB as authoritative state
177.    - Keep registration submission records durable until resolved/cancelled
178.    - Use realtime messages for coordination signals, then persist resulting state transitions
179.    - Ensure API remains policy judge for worker health/compatibility decisions
180.    - Keep schema minimal (no heartbeat audit log retention for this subsystem)
181.    - Keep transport-facing code behind an explicit realtime transport abstraction (no direct PG wiring in orchestration logic)
182. 
183. 4. Lifecycle and failure semantics:
184.    - Define deterministic request/response envelopes with correlation IDs and idempotency keys
185.    - Add explicit timeout/retry policy for dropped or delayed notifications
186.    - Ensure timeout cleanup prevents stale submissions from being approved after worker process is gone
187.    - Treat force-deregistered epochs as invalid forever; worker must re-register to obtain a new epoch
188.    - Define worker-side shutdown handling so API invalidation drives explicit non-zero process exit when worker is still running
189.    - Map failures to typed classes (`transient` vs `terminal`) and deterministic worker exit behavior
190. 
191. 5. Verification:
192.     - Add integration coverage for:
193.        - startup compatibility accept path
194.        - startup compatibility reject path (worker exits non-zero)
195.           - startup compatibility timeout path (worker revokes pending submission and exits non-zero)
196.           - API restart catch-up path (processes pending DB submissions created before API became available)
197.        - API heartbeat request loop over `LISTEN/NOTIFY`
198.     - missed heartbeat proof handling causes force deregistration for the targeted epoch
199.     - live worker receives shutdown intent and exits non-zero
200.     - restarted worker after invalidation must submit new registration and cannot reuse old epoch
201.     - transport abstraction conformance tests (shared contract tests runnable against PG implementation)
202.     - Run full `go test ./...`
203.

 # TODO: Worker Supervisor Scheduler Dependency
 
 ## Discovery Summary
 
 The worker runtime still depends on `taskengine.Scheduler` for supervisor-driven enqueue flows, even after splitting Asynq into API/Worker platforms.
 
 ### Where this dependency exists
 
 - `internal/core/worker/worker.go`
   - Bootstraps scheduler via `taskengine.NewScheduler(...)`
   - Wires `SetAdmissionLedger(...)` and `SetAdmissionSignalSink(...)`
   - Passes scheduler into `applicationcontrolplane.NewService(...)`

 - `internal/infrastructure/supervisor/taskengine/dispatcher.go`
   - Uses scheduler to enqueue follow-up jobs from supervisor decisions (`scheduler.Enqueue(...)`)
 
 - `internal/application/controlplane/documents.go`
   - Uses scheduler for project document task enqueue paths when service is constructed in worker runtime
 
 ## Why this matters
 
 Goal is to keep worker as a pure Asynq consumer/processor. Current scheduler usage means worker can still originate enqueue operations in some flows.
 
 ## Follow-up tasks
 
 1. Define target boundary:
    - API is sole enqueue/admission owner
    - Worker is consumer-only (register/start/shutdown handlers)
 
 2. Remove worker-side enqueue origins:
    - Refactor supervisor dispatcher usage so enqueue decisions are executed by API-side orchestration
    - Prevent worker-constructed control-plane service from owning enqueue paths
 
 3. Update worker bootstrap:
    - Remove scheduler creation from worker runtime once enqueue paths are removed
    - Keep only `WorkerPlatform` consumer lifecycle (`Register`, `Start`, `Shutdown`)
 
 4. Preserve distributed contract:
    - Ensure any handoff from worker observations to API enqueue remains via persisted/distributed mechanisms
 
 5. Verification:
    - Add/adjust tests proving worker no longer calls `scheduler.Enqueue(...)`
    - Run full `go test ./...`
 
 ---
 
 # TODO: Realtime Worker Registry + Heartbeat Refactor (LISTEN/NOTIFY First)
 
 ## Status
 
 - Current: completed
 - End state target: completed
 - Completion gate: do not mark completed until desktop + API + worker compile successfully and full project tests pass again
 
 ## Scope mandate
 
 This is a complete refactor/rewrite of the worker registration + heartbeat subsystem.
 
 - Do not deliver this as a shim, bridge layer, or minimal tactical patch
 - Do not preserve old polling/legacy lifecycle behavior behind feature toggles
 - Replace the existing flow with the new realtime-first model as the primary architecture
 - Remove superseded paths once replacement behavior is verified
 - Breaking changes are explicitly accepted for this refactor
 - No migration path is required for previous worker heartbeat/registration flow
 - No backward-compatibility guarantees are required for legacy contracts in this subsystem
 
 ## End-to-end implementation plan
 
 ### 1) Domain contracts and lifecycle model
 - [ ] Define durable registration submission domain contracts (request, response, status, reason, timeout, revoke)
 - [ ] Define realtime heartbeat request/response contracts (API initiated, worker response, deadline semantics)
 - [ ] Define explicit worker lifecycle transitions for new model (`pending_registration`, `registered`, `healthy`, `invalidated`, `deregistered`)
 - [ ] Remove/replace any remaining legacy state semantics not used by the new flow
 
 ### 2) Infrastructure persistence (minimal Postgres)
 - [ ] Add minimal durable registration submission persistence (create/list pending/update status/revoke)
 - [ ] Keep worker registry persistence minimal and aligned with new lifecycle only
 - [ ] Remove obsolete persistence fields and write paths that are no longer reused
 - [ ] Ensure API startup catch-up query path processes pending registration submissions
 
 ### 3) Realtime transport abstraction and PG implementation
 - [ ] Keep/establish transport abstraction at domain/application boundaries
 - [ ] Implement/align PostgreSQL LISTEN/NOTIFY adapter for registration + heartbeat channels
 - [ ] Ensure deterministic envelopes (correlation IDs + idempotency keys)
 - [ ] Ensure transport usage remains swappable for future non-PG backend
 
 ### 4) API runtime implementation
 - [ ] API listens for registration submissions in realtime and judges compatibility
 - [ ] API responds accept/reject in realtime and persists outcome atomically
 - [ ] API periodically initiates heartbeat requests to registered/healthy workers via realtime transport
 - [ ] API enforces deadline, invalidates missed workers, and persists deregistration/invalidation
 - [ ] API startup catch-up handles pending submissions created while API was down
 
 ### 5) Worker runtime implementation
 - [ ] Worker startup creates durable registration submission + realtime notify
 - [ ] Worker waits for API decision with bounded timeout/retry policy
 - [ ] Worker revokes pending submission on timeout before exiting non-zero
 - [ ] Worker exits non-zero on reject and logs explicit reasons
 - [ ] Worker handles API heartbeat requests and responds with health proof
 - [ ] Worker handles API invalidation/shutdown intent and exits non-zero when live
 - [ ] Worker restart always performs fresh registration; old epoch/session never reused
 
 ### 6) Desktop scope (frontend)
 - [ ] Update desktop worker-related GraphQL operations/models to new contract shape
 - [ ] Preserve desktop realtime visibility for registered worker count and worker session information
 - [ ] Remove desktop dependencies on deleted legacy fields/events
 - [ ] Ensure desktop builds cleanly with updated schema/generated artifacts
 
 ### 7) GraphQL/schema/codegen reconciliation
 - [ ] Finalize GraphQL schema for worker sessions/settings/events under new model
 - [ ] Regenerate GraphQL server artifacts and frontend/client artifacts
 - [ ] Remove stale generated code and stale resolver paths no longer used
 
 ### 8) Test strategy and required coverage
 - [ ] Add API tests: registration accept/reject, startup catch-up, heartbeat request loop, invalidation on missed proof
 - [ ] Add worker tests: timeout revoke path, reject non-zero path, heartbeat response path, shutdown intent non-zero exit path
 - [ ] Add transport tests: abstraction conformance + PG adapter behavior
 - [ ] Add persistence tests: submission durability, pending query, status transitions, revoke semantics
 - [ ] Add desktop tests for worker realtime/session/count rendering against new contract
 - [ ] Remove obsolete tests from deleted legacy behavior
 
 ### 9) Compile and verification gate
 - [x] API compile/build green
 - [x] Worker compile/build green
 - [x] Desktop compile/build green
 - [x] Full backend test suite green (`go test ./...`)
 - [x] Frontend test suite green (desktop/frontend tests)
 - [ ] End-to-end regression passes for registration + heartbeat lifecycle
 
 ### 10) Completion status update
 - [x] Update this TODO status from `implementing` to `completed` only after all compile + test gates above pass
 
 ## Target behavior
 
 Workers rely on API-owned runtime configuration persisted in the database, and worker registration records remain persisted in the database. Coordination and liveness signaling move to a realtime-first PostgreSQL `LISTEN/NOTIFY` pipeline.
 
 Registration compatibility requests must be durable: each worker startup request is persisted in the database and also emitted through realtime `NOTIFY` for low-latency handling.
 
 Heartbeat policy is API-initiated and proof-based:
 - Workers may claim `healthy`, but API is the authority that periodically requests heartbeat proofs from currently `registered`/`healthy` workers
 - Missing/failed proofs cause API-side force deregistration of that worker epoch
 - If the worker process is still alive, it must receive shutdown intent and exit with non-zero status
 - If the worker was offline during invalidation and later restarts, it must submit a new registration; old epoch/registration is invalid
 
 Postgres persistence for this subsystem should be minimal:
 - Keep authoritative current worker registry state and runtime settings
 - Keep durable registration submission records needed for startup compatibility handshake/replay
 - Do not add heartbeat audit history retention tables/logs for this flow
 
 Realtime transport architecture must remain swappable:
 - Treat realtime heartbeat/registration messaging as an abstraction at domain/application boundaries
 - Provide a concrete PostgreSQL `LISTEN/NOTIFY` implementation for now
 - If abstraction and concrete implementation are not both present, add them as part of this refactor
 - Keep contracts deterministic so transport can be replaced later (for example, a stronger broker) without changing core orchestration logic
 
 ## Refactor requirements
 
 1. Realtime registration compatibility handshake:
    - On worker startup, persist a registration compatibility submission record in DB and publish a corresponding realtime `NOTIFY` event
    - API consumes request, validates worker build/runtime compatibility (version + remote backend/runtime config expectations)
    - API returns accept/reject response over the same realtime pipeline
    - API startup path must scan and process pending registration submissions from DB (for requests created while API was unavailable)
    - Worker must not register in DB unless API explicitly responds with `ok`
    - Worker waits for response with bounded timeout; on timeout worker revokes/cancels its pending DB submission and exits non-zero
    - Worker exits non-zero on explicit reject and logs reject reasons at error level
 
 2. API-initiated heartbeat proving:
    - API is the initiator and periodically requests heartbeats from all workers currently marked `registered`/`healthy`
    - Requests and responses flow through `LISTEN/NOTIFY` (no polling-first model)
    - Worker responds to API heartbeat request with proof-of-health payload
    - On missed response deadline, API force-deregisters the worker epoch as invalid
    - API emits shutdown intent/event for live workers so runtime terminates with non-zero exit
 
 3. DB as source of truth + realtime as transport:
    - Keep worker registry/settings in DB as authoritative state
    - Keep registration submission records durable until resolved/cancelled
    - Use realtime messages for coordination signals, then persist resulting state transitions
    - Ensure API remains policy judge for worker health/compatibility decisions
    - Keep schema minimal (no heartbeat audit log retention for this subsystem)
    - Keep transport-facing code behind an explicit realtime transport abstraction (no direct PG wiring in orchestration logic)
 
 4. Lifecycle and failure semantics:
    - Define deterministic request/response envelopes with correlation IDs and idempotency keys
    - Add explicit timeout/retry policy for dropped or delayed notifications
    - Ensure timeout cleanup prevents stale submissions from being approved after worker process is gone
    - Treat force-deregistered epochs as invalid forever; worker must re-register to obtain a new epoch
    - Define worker-side shutdown handling so API invalidation drives explicit non-zero process exit when worker is still running
    - Map failures to typed classes (`transient` vs `terminal`) and deterministic worker exit behavior
 
 5. Verification:
     - Add integration coverage for:
        - startup compatibility accept path
        - startup compatibility reject path (worker exits non-zero)
           - startup compatibility timeout path (worker revokes pending submission and exits non-zero)
           - API restart catch-up path (processes pending DB submissions created before API became available)
        - API heartbeat request loop over `LISTEN/NOTIFY`
     - missed heartbeat proof handling causes force deregistration for the targeted epoch
     - live worker receives shutdown intent and exits non-zero
     - restarted worker after invalidation must submit new registration and cannot reuse old epoch
     - transport abstraction conformance tests (shared contract tests runnable against PG implementation)
     - Run full `go test ./...`

 # V1 Scope Boundaries
 
 ## Purpose
 
 Define what V1 includes, what V1 excludes, and the constraints all implementation work must follow.
 
 ## Rewrite Policy (Mandatory)
 
 V1 is a ground-up rewrite contained in `v1/`.
 
 - `mvp/` is reference and inspiration only.
 - No source import, package reuse, file copy, or code migration from `mvp/` into `v1/`.
 - V1 contracts, schemas, adapters, and flows are authored natively in V1.
 
 ## In Scope (V1)
 
 - GraphQL-first control plane using `gqlgen`.
 - Cross-platform client as the end-user operating surface.
 - Local and remote worker execution with SCM-backed bootstrap.
 - Slot model with canonical boundaries: `agent`, `scm`, `tracker`, `notifier`, `client`.
 - Tracker-agnostic taskboard model with local JSON provider first.
 - Realtime streams for session activity, agent output, and orchestrator decisions.
 - Container-first runtime and deployment baseline.
 
 ## Out of Scope / Non-Goals (V1)
 
 - Terminal-app product UX.

 ## Scope Gates for New Work
 
 A new item is in scope only if it satisfies all checks:
 
 1. Fits one or more V1 in-scope capabilities above.

 3. Supports or does not block GraphQL-first control plane operation.

1. # Version 1 Roadmap (High-Level)
2. 
3. ## Purpose
4. 
5. Define the high-level path to V1 release and the release-level outcomes that must be achieved.
6. 
7. Detailed execution plans belong in `docs/roadmap/*`.
8. 
9. ## V1 Mission
10. 
11. Ship a GraphQL-first orchestrator platform with local + remote workers, real-time operational streams, tracker-agnostic planning, and a cross-platform client experience, all under strict DDD boundaries, container-first runtime parity, and PostgreSQL as the durable system of record.
12. 
13. ## Non-Negotiable Constraints
14. 
15. - V1 is a ground-up rewrite in `v1/`.
16. - `mvp/` is inspiration only; no copy/migrate/reuse of source.
17. - Canonical slots: `agent`, `scm`, `tracker`, `notifier`, `client`.
18. - Dependency direction: `interface -> application -> domain` (infrastructure implements inner-layer ports).
19. - End-user product UX is client-first (not terminal/tmux/CLI).
20. - Containerized runtime is the default deployable contract.
21. - PostgreSQL is the durable source of truth for business/operational state.
22. - Redis is limited to queue transport internals for `asynq`.
23. 
24. ## Release Pillars
25. 
26. 1. **Architecture Foundation**
27.    - Enforced placement rules and slot boundaries.
28.    - Stable composition roots and runtime contracts.
29.    - Explicit persistence placement rules by layer.
30. 
31. 2. **Execution Plane**
32.    - Local and remote worker parity.
33.    - SCM-backed remote bootstrap and resumable checkpoints.
34.    - Durable checkpoint/journal/lease/worker-registry state in Postgres.
35. 
36. 3. **Control Plane**
37.    - GraphQL schema as primary contract.
38.    - Queries, mutations, and subscriptions for orchestrator operations.
39.    - Postgres-backed query/read models for run/task/job/worker history.
40. 
41. 4. **Tracker-Agnostic Planning**
42.    - Canonical taskboard domain model.
43.    - Local JSON adapter + external provider extension boundary.
44.    - Postgres canonical tracker persistence (snapshots + normalized relational model).
45. 
46. 5. **Realtime Observability and Streams**
47.    - Session activity, agent output, supervisor/orchestrator decision streams.
48.    - Correlation IDs through execution and events.
49.    - Postgres-backed stream replay storage for reconnect diagnostics.
50. 
51. 6. **Cross-Platform Client**
52.    - Runtime-configurable GraphQL endpoint.
53.    - Operational dashboard + session detail/controls.
54.    - Live + historical operational visibility from persisted control-plane models.
55. 
56. 7. **Container-First Delivery**
57.    - API and worker container images.
58.    - Compose-based local parity and release-ready artifact flow.
59.    - Postgres migration/init lifecycle included in deployment contract.
60. 
61. ## High-Level Delivery Phases
62. 
63. ### Phase 0 — Foundation
64. 
65. - Finalize scope and placement governance.
66. - Establish baseline project layout and startup lifecycle contracts.
67. - Lock persistence baseline (Postgres durable state, Redis queue transport only).
68. 
69. ### Phase 1 — Core Runtime Contracts (SCM/Agent/Worker First)
70. 
71. - Define `agent` and `scm` slot interfaces with initial adapters.
72. - Define worker lease/capability and execution contracts.
73. - Validate SCM-backed execution baseline before supervisor policy depth.
74. - Implement durable execution reliability persistence (checkpoint, execution journal, admission ledger, dead-letter triage, lease records, worker registry).
75. 
76. ### Phase 2 — Orchestrator + GraphQL Core
77. 
78. - Implement supervisor state/event taxonomy over real execution context.
79. - Persist supervisor decision history in Postgres.
80. - Establish GraphQL contract surface and core control actions backed by application/query services.
81. 
82. ### Phase 3 — Tracker + Realtime + Client Surface
83. 
84. - Deliver canonical tracker/taskboard ingestion flows with durable persistence.
85. - Extend tracker persistence from snapshots to normalized relational model.
86. - Deliver subscription/event stream pathways with persisted replay offsets.
87. - Deliver first usable cross-platform client flows with replay-aware resilience.
88. 
89. ### Phase 4 — Release Hardening
90. 
91. - Container/runtime parity validation.
92. - Postgres schema migration/versioning hardening.
93. - Reliability, operability, and release criteria closure.
94. 
95. ## Current Persistence Status (Aligned to Slice Plans)
96. 
97. - **Implemented now**: Postgres client/lifecycle wiring, checkpoint persistence, execution journal persistence, SCM lease persistence, worker registry persistence, tracker board snapshot persistence, normalized tracker relational persistence (`tracker_boards`, `tracker_epics`, `tracker_tasks`, `tracker_task_outcomes`), admission ledger enqueue writes, dead-letter requeue audit events, supervisor decision history persistence (`supervisor_events`).
98. - **Next persistence targets**: lease expiry/stale recovery, session snapshots, stream replay store, GraphQL read models.
99. 
100. ## V1 Release Exit Criteria
101. 
102. V1 is release-ready only when all are true:
103. 
104. 1. GraphQL control plane supports core orchestrator workflows.
105. 2. Local and remote worker modes both execute via shared orchestration contracts.
106. 3. Realtime session/agent/supervisor streams are available live and replayable from persisted storage.
107. 4. Tracker flows operate on canonical internal model with durable Postgres backing.
108. 5. Cross-platform client can operate the system without terminal-first UX.
109. 6. API and worker are containerized and validated under compose-based runtime profiles with Postgres initialization/migration lifecycle.
110. 7. Postgres is authoritative for business-critical and operationally relevant state; Redis remains queue transport only.
111. 8. DDD boundary and placement constraints are maintained across implementation.
112. 
113. ## Risks to Track
114. 
115. - Slot-boundary leakage under delivery pressure.
116. - Remote worker bootstrap complexity (SCM auth, checkout, resume fidelity).
117. - Incomplete persistence coverage causing split-brain operational state.
118. - Stream volume/performance under concurrent sessions.
119. - Drift between API contract and client expectations.
120. - Host-native shortcuts eroding container-first parity.
121. 
122. ## Immediate Priority Focus
123. 
124. 1. Complete remaining persistence conversions identified in `docs/roadmap/09-postgres-persistence-conversion.md`.
125. 2. Land stream persistence (`stream_events`) and replay-path wiring before full client polish.
126. 3. Back GraphQL query/subscription surfaces with Postgres read models.
127. 4. Add formal schema migration/versioning and deployment lifecycle checks.
128.

 - Finalize scope and placement governance.
 - Establish baseline project layout and startup lifecycle contracts.
 - Lock persistence baseline (Postgres durable state, Redis queue transport only).
 
 ### Phase 1 — Core Runtime Contracts (SCM/Agent/Worker First)
 
 - Define `agent` and `scm` slot interfaces with initial adapters.
 - Define worker lease/capability and execution contracts.
 - Validate SCM-backed execution baseline before supervisor policy depth.
 - Implement durable execution reliability persistence (checkpoint, execution journal, admission ledger, dead-letter triage, lease records, worker registry).
 
 ### Phase 2 — Orchestrator + GraphQL Core
 
 - Implement supervisor state/event taxonomy over real execution context.
 - Persist supervisor decision history in Postgres.
 - Establish GraphQL contract surface and core control actions backed by application/query services.
 
 ### Phase 3 — Tracker + Realtime + Client Surface
 
 - Deliver canonical tracker/taskboard ingestion flows with durable persistence.
 - Extend tracker persistence from snapshots to normalized relational model.
 - Deliver subscription/event stream pathways with persisted replay offsets.
 - Deliver first usable cross-platform client flows with replay-aware resilience.
 
 ### Phase 4 — Release Hardening
 
 - Container/runtime parity validation.
 - Postgres schema migration/versioning hardening.
 - Reliability, operability, and release criteria closure.
 
 ## Current Persistence Status (Aligned to Slice Plans)
 
 - **Implemented now**: Postgres client/lifecycle wiring, checkpoint persistence, execution journal persistence, SCM lease persistence, worker registry persistence, tracker board snapshot persistence, normalized tracker relational persistence (`tracker_boards`, `tracker_epics`, `tracker_tasks`, `tracker_task_outcomes`), admission ledger enqueue writes, dead-letter requeue audit events, supervisor decision history persistence (`supervisor_events`).
 - **Next persistence targets**: lease expiry/stale recovery, session snapshots, stream replay store, GraphQL read models.
 
 ## V1 Release Exit Criteria
 
 V1 is release-ready only when all are true:
 
 1. GraphQL control plane supports core orchestrator workflows.
 2. Local and remote worker modes both execute via shared orchestration contracts.
 3. Realtime session/agent/supervisor streams are available live and replayable from persisted storage.
 4. Tracker flows operate on canonical internal model with durable Postgres backing.
 5. Cross-platform client can operate the system without terminal-first UX.
 6. API and worker are containerized and validated under compose-based runtime profiles with Postgres initialization/migration lifecycle.
 7. Postgres is authoritative for business-critical and operationally relevant state; Redis remains queue transport only.
 8. DDD boundary and placement constraints are maintained across implementation.
 
 ## Risks to Track
 
 - Slot-boundary leakage under delivery pressure.
 - Remote worker bootstrap complexity (SCM auth, checkout, resume fidelity).
 - Incomplete persistence coverage causing split-brain operational state.
 - Stream volume/performance under concurrent sessions.
 - Drift between API contract and client expectations.
 - Host-native shortcuts eroding container-first parity.
 
 ## Immediate Priority Focus
 
 1. Complete remaining persistence conversions identified in `docs/roadmap/09-postgres-persistence-conversion.md`.
 2. Land stream persistence (`stream_events`) and replay-path wiring before full client polish.
 3. Back GraphQL query/subscription surfaces with Postgres read models.
 4. Add formal schema migration/versioning and deployment lifecycle checks.

1. # Tracker Domain V1
2. 
3. ## Goal
4. 
5. Define a brand new Project Board domain model for V1 with three Postgres tables:
6. 
7. - project_boards
8. - project_board_epics
9. - project_board_tasks
10. 
11. This model treats the board as the aggregate root and requires task-level model audit + outcome persistence.
12. 
13. ## Domain Model
14. 
15. ### Aggregate: ProjectBoard
16. 
17. The board is the root entity for planning and execution tracking per project.
18. 
19. Core properties:

79. 
80. ### project_boards
81. 
82. Columns:
83. 
84. - id uuid primary key
85. - project_id uuid not null
86. - name text not null
87. - state text not null
88. - created_at timestamptz not null default now()
89. - updated_at timestamptz not null default now()

94. - index idx_project_boards_project_id (project_id)
95. - index idx_project_boards_updated_at (updated_at desc)
96. 
97. ### project_board_epics
98. 
99. Columns:
100. 
101. - id uuid primary key
102. - board_id uuid not null references project_boards(id) on delete cascade
103. - title text not null
104. - objective text
105. - state text not null

114. - index idx_project_board_epics_board_id (board_id)
115. - index idx_project_board_epics_state (state)
116. - index idx_project_board_epics_depends_on_epic_ids (depends_on_epic_ids) using gin
117.

119. 
120. Columns:
121. 
122. - id uuid primary key
123. - board_id uuid not null references project_boards(id) on delete cascade

156.

163. 
164. - unique (epic_id, rank)
165. - index idx_project_board_tasks_board_id (board_id)
166. - index idx_project_board_tasks_epic_id (epic_id)
167. - index idx_project_board_tasks_state (state)
168. - index idx_project_board_tasks_outcome_status (outcome_status)
169. - index idx_project_board_tasks_model_run_id (model_run_id)
170. - index idx_project_board_tasks_depends_on_task_ids (depends_on_task_ids) using gin
171. - index idx_project_board_tasks_ready_scan (board_id, state, epic_id, rank)
172. - index idx_project_board_tasks_claim_expiry (claim_expires_at)
173. 
174. ## Mandatory Invariants
175. 
176. - Task must reference a valid board and epic.
177. - Task.board_id must match Epic.board_id.
178. - model_provider + model_name are required for any model-produced task result.
179. - outcome_status is always required.
180. - If outcome_status = 'failed', outcome_error_code is required.
181. - A task can be claimed by at most one active worker lease at a time.
182. - Dependency-gated tasks are not claimable until all prerequisite tasks are in terminal-unblocking state.
183. 
184. ## Atomic NextTask Scheduling (DAG + Fan-Out/Fan-In)
185. 
186. ### Why
187. 
188. - Many concurrent agents must request work without claiming the same task.
189. - Task dispatch must respect task and epic dependency DAG constraints.
190. - Selection order must be deterministic: epic rank first, then task rank.
191. 
192. ### NextTask contract
193. 
194. - Input: `board_id`, `agent_id`, `lease_ttl`
195. - Output: one claimed task or no task available
196. - Atomicity: select + claim happen in one transaction
197. 
198. ### Eligibility rules
199. 
200. A task is eligible for claim only if all are true:
201. 
202. - `task.state = 'planned'`
203. - task is not currently claimed, or claim lease expired (`claim_expires_at <= now()`)
204. - all `depends_on_task_ids` tasks are terminal-unblocking (`completed` or `no_work_needed`)
205. - task's epic has no unresolved epic dependency lock
206. 
207. Epic dependency lock rule:
208. 
209. - An epic is runnable only when all epics in `depends_on_epic_ids` are `completed`.
210. 
211. ### Deterministic ordering
212. 
213. - Order by `project_board_epics.rank ASC`, then `project_board_tasks.rank ASC`
214. 
215. ### Atomic claim pattern (Postgres)
216. 
217. Use a single transaction with row locking:
218. 
219. 1. Find first eligible task by rank order.
220. 2. Lock candidate row with `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`.
221. 3. Update claim fields (`claimed_by_agent_id`, `claimed_at`, `claim_expires_at`, `claim_token`, `state='in_progress'`, `attempt_count=attempt_count+1`).
222. 4. Commit and return claimed task.
223. 
224. This guarantees agent1 gets task1, agent2 gets task2, etc. without duplicate claims.
225. 
226. ### Fan-out behavior
227. 
228. - Every agent calls `NextTask` asynchronously.
229. - If tasks are available, calls distribute work across many agents concurrently.
230. 
231. ### Fan-in behavior
232. 
233. - If no task is currently eligible, return `no_task_available` (not error).
234. - Agents backoff/jitter and retry, or wake via event/notification when dependency state changes.
235. - Completion of prerequisite tasks naturally unlocks dependent tasks for future `NextTask` calls.
236. 
237. ### Lease and recovery rules
238. 
239. - If an agent crashes, expired lease makes task reclaimable.
240. - Optional heartbeat may extend `claim_expires_at` for long-running tasks.
241. - A completion/failure write must validate `claim_token` so only the current holder can finalize.
242. 
243. ### Ingestion retry continuity (same asynq task invocation)
244. 
245. - Taskboard generation/validation retries occur inside one asynq task invocation (no handoff to a new worker task for each validation retry).
246. - Retry attempts must reuse and carry forward available run continuity metadata (`agent_session_id`, `agent_stream_id`).
247. - Persist the latest known continuity metadata on the task model audit so resume/follow-up calls can continue the same agent stream/session when supported.
248. 
249. ## State Transition Guidance
250. 
251. - board_state: pending -> active -> completed | failed
252. - epic_state: planned -> in_progress -> completed | blocked | failed
253. - task_state: planned -> in_progress -> completed | failed | no_work_needed
254. - outcome_status: success | partial | failed
255. 
256. ## Implementation Notes
257. 
258. - Keep orchestration in application layer.
259. - Keep invariants and transitions in domain layer.
260. - Keep Postgres-specific details in infrastructure repositories.
261. - Keep API/worker correlation identifiers persisted via model_run_id and upstream run/task/job IDs.
262. 
263. ## Scope Guardrails (Explicit)
264. 
265. - Ignore redesign of already-implemented agent task execution internals.
266. - Existing agent task execution code should only be touched enough to compile against this new contract.
267. - Primary focus is wiring, the new tracker domain contract, and the atomic fan-in/fan-out `NextTask` scheduler.
268. - Do not introduce additional Postgres tables for this tracker rewrite unless absolutely necessary.
269. - Prefer extending the three existing tracker tables and their columns/indexes over adding new tables.
270. 
271. ## Go Domain Struct Model
272. 
273. ```go
274. package domain
275. 
276. import (
277.  "time"
278. 
279.  "github.com/google/uuid"
280. )
281. 
282. type BoardState string
283. 
284. const (
285.  BoardStatePending   BoardState = "pending"
286.  BoardStateActive    BoardState = "active"
287.  BoardStateCompleted BoardState = "completed"
288.  BoardStateFailed    BoardState = "failed"
289. )
290. 
291. type EpicState string
292. 
293. const (
294.  EpicStatePlanned    EpicState = "planned"
295.  EpicStateInProgress EpicState = "in_progress"
296.  EpicStateCompleted  EpicState = "completed"
297.  EpicStateBlocked    EpicState = "blocked"
298.  EpicStateFailed     EpicState = "failed"
299. )
300. 
301. type TaskState string
302. 
303. const (
304.  TaskStatePlanned    TaskState = "planned"
305.  TaskStateInProgress TaskState = "in_progress"
306.  TaskStateCompleted  TaskState = "completed"
307.  TaskStateFailed     TaskState = "failed"
308.  TaskStateNoWorkNeeded TaskState = "no_work_needed"
309. )
310. 
311. type OutcomeStatus string
312. 
313. const (
314.  OutcomeStatusSuccess OutcomeStatus = "success"
315.  OutcomeStatusPartial OutcomeStatus = "partial"
316.  OutcomeStatusFailed  OutcomeStatus = "failed"
317. )
318. 
319. type ProjectBoard struct {
320.  ID        uuid.UUID
321.  ProjectID uuid.UUID
322.  Name      string
323.  State     BoardState
324.  CreatedAt time.Time
325.  UpdatedAt time.Time
326. }
327. 
328. type ProjectBoardEpic struct {
329.  ID        uuid.UUID
330.  BoardID   uuid.UUID
331.  Title     string
332.  Objective *string
333.  State     EpicState
334.  Rank      int
335.  DependsOnEpicIDs []uuid.UUID
336.  CreatedAt time.Time
337.  UpdatedAt time.Time
338. }
339. 
340. type TaskModelAudit struct {
341.  ModelProvider     string
342.  ModelName         string
343.  ModelVersion      *string
344.  ModelRunID        *uuid.UUID
345. 	AgentSessionID    *string
346. 	AgentStreamID     *string
347.  PromptFingerprint *string
348.  InputTokens       *int
349.  OutputTokens      *int
350.  StartedAt         *time.Time
351.  CompletedAt       *time.Time
352. }
353. 
354. type TaskOutcome struct {
355.  Status       OutcomeStatus
356.  Summary      string
357.  ErrorCode    *string
358.  ErrorMessage *string
359. }
360. 
361. type ProjectBoardTask struct {
362.  ID        uuid.UUID
363.  BoardID   uuid.UUID
364.  EpicID    uuid.UUID
365.  Title     string
366.  TaskType  string
367.  State     TaskState
368.  Rank      int
369.  DependsOnTaskIDs []uuid.UUID
370.  Audit     TaskModelAudit
371.  Outcome   TaskOutcome
372.  CreatedAt time.Time
373.  UpdatedAt time.Time
374. }
375. ```
376. 
377. ### Validation expectations for the Go model
378. 
379. - `ProjectBoardTask.BoardID` must match the owning epic `BoardID`.
380. - `ProjectBoardEpic.DependsOnEpicIDs` can only contain epics from the same board and cannot include itself.
381. - `ProjectBoardTask.DependsOnTaskIDs` can only contain tasks from the same board and cannot include itself.
382. - `TaskOutcome.Status` is always required.
383. - `TaskOutcome.Summary` is the only outcome content field (no large payload/blob field).
384. - `TaskModelAudit.ModelProvider` and `TaskModelAudit.ModelName` are required for model-produced results.
385. - `TaskModelAudit.AgentSessionID` and `TaskModelAudit.AgentStreamID` are optional but should be set when the agent runtime provides resumable session/stream identifiers.
386. - If `TaskOutcome.Status == OutcomeStatusFailed`, `TaskOutcome.ErrorCode` must be present.
387.

@@ -1,387 +1,387 @@
 # Tracker Domain V1
 
 ## Goal
 
 Define a brand new Project Board domain model for V1 with three Postgres tables:
 
 - project_boards
 - project_board_epics
 - project_board_tasks
 
 This model treats the board as the aggregate root and requires task-level model audit + outcome persistence.
 
 ## Domain Model
 
 ### Aggregate: ProjectBoard
 
 The board is the root entity for planning and execution tracking per project.
 
 Core properties:
 
 - board_id
 - project_id
 - name
 - board_state (pending, active, completed, failed)
 - created_at
 - updated_at
 
 Rules:
 
 - A project can have one active board at a time.
 - Board deletion cascades to epics and tasks.
 
 ### Entity: ProjectBoardEpic
 
 An epic is a board-scoped grouping of related tasks.
 
 Core properties:
 
 - epic_id
 - board_id
 - title
 - objective
 - epic_state (planned, in_progress, completed, blocked, failed)
 - rank
 - depends_on_epic_ids
 - created_at
 - updated_at
 
 Rules:
 
 - Every epic belongs to exactly one board.
 - Epic rank is unique within a board for deterministic ordering.
 - Epic dependencies can only reference epics in the same board.
 
 ### Entity: ProjectBoardTask
 
 A task is an executable work unit under an epic and stores AI/model audit + outcome.
 
 Core properties:
 
 - task_id
 - board_id
 - epic_id
 - title
 - task_type
 - task_state (planned, in_progress, completed, failed, no_work_needed)
 - rank
 - depends_on_task_ids
 - created_at
 - updated_at
 
 Rules:
 
 - Every task belongs to exactly one epic and one board.
 - Task board_id must equal the board_id of its epic.
 - Task dependencies can only reference tasks in the same board.
 
 ## Postgres Schema Contract
 
 ### project_boards
 
 Columns:
 
 - id uuid primary key
 - project_id uuid not null
 - name text not null
 - state text not null
 - created_at timestamptz not null default now()
 - updated_at timestamptz not null default now()
 
 Constraints / indexes:
 
 - unique (project_id, state) where state = 'active'
 - index idx_project_boards_project_id (project_id)
 - index idx_project_boards_updated_at (updated_at desc)
 
 ### project_board_epics
 
 Columns:
 
 - id uuid primary key
 - board_id uuid not null references project_boards(id) on delete cascade
 - title text not null
 - objective text
 - state text not null
 - rank int not null
 - depends_on_epic_ids uuid[] not null default '{}'
 - created_at timestamptz not null default now()
 - updated_at timestamptz not null default now()
 
 Constraints / indexes:
 
 - unique (board_id, rank)
 - index idx_project_board_epics_board_id (board_id)
 - index idx_project_board_epics_state (state)
 - index idx_project_board_epics_depends_on_epic_ids (depends_on_epic_ids) using gin
 
 ### project_board_tasks
 
 Columns:
 
 - id uuid primary key
 - board_id uuid not null references project_boards(id) on delete cascade
 - epic_id uuid not null references project_board_epics(id) on delete cascade
 - title text not null
 - task_type text not null
 - state text not null
 - rank int not null
 - depends_on_task_ids uuid[] not null default '{}'
 - claimed_by_agent_id text
 - claimed_at timestamptz
 - claim_expires_at timestamptz
 - claim_token uuid
 - attempt_count int not null default 0
 
 Model audit columns:
 
 - model_provider text not null
 - model_name text not null
 - model_version text
 - model_run_id uuid
 - agent_session_id text
 - agent_stream_id text
 - prompt_fingerprint text
 - input_tokens int
 - output_tokens int
 - started_at timestamptz
 - completed_at timestamptz
 
 Outcome columns:
 
 - outcome_status text not null
 - outcome_summary text not null
 - outcome_error_code text
 - outcome_error_message text
 
 Lifecycle columns:
 
 - created_at timestamptz not null default now()
 - updated_at timestamptz not null default now()
 
 Constraints / indexes:
 
 - unique (epic_id, rank)
 - index idx_project_board_tasks_board_id (board_id)
 - index idx_project_board_tasks_epic_id (epic_id)
 - index idx_project_board_tasks_state (state)
 - index idx_project_board_tasks_outcome_status (outcome_status)
 - index idx_project_board_tasks_model_run_id (model_run_id)
 - index idx_project_board_tasks_depends_on_task_ids (depends_on_task_ids) using gin
 - index idx_project_board_tasks_ready_scan (board_id, state, epic_id, rank)
 - index idx_project_board_tasks_claim_expiry (claim_expires_at)
 
 ## Mandatory Invariants
 
 - Task must reference a valid board and epic.
 - Task.board_id must match Epic.board_id.
 - model_provider + model_name are required for any model-produced task result.
 - outcome_status is always required.
 - If outcome_status = 'failed', outcome_error_code is required.
 - A task can be claimed by at most one active worker lease at a time.
 - Dependency-gated tasks are not claimable until all prerequisite tasks are in terminal-unblocking state.
 
 ## Atomic NextTask Scheduling (DAG + Fan-Out/Fan-In)
 
 ### Why
 
 - Many concurrent agents must request work without claiming the same task.
 - Task dispatch must respect task and epic dependency DAG constraints.
 - Selection order must be deterministic: epic rank first, then task rank.
 
 ### NextTask contract
 
 - Input: `board_id`, `agent_id`, `lease_ttl`
 - Output: one claimed task or no task available
 - Atomicity: select + claim happen in one transaction
 
 ### Eligibility rules
 
 A task is eligible for claim only if all are true:
 
 - `task.state = 'planned'`
 - task is not currently claimed, or claim lease expired (`claim_expires_at <= now()`)
 - all `depends_on_task_ids` tasks are terminal-unblocking (`completed` or `no_work_needed`)
 - task's epic has no unresolved epic dependency lock
 
 Epic dependency lock rule:
 
 - An epic is runnable only when all epics in `depends_on_epic_ids` are `completed`.
 
 ### Deterministic ordering
 
 - Order by `project_board_epics.rank ASC`, then `project_board_tasks.rank ASC`
 
 ### Atomic claim pattern (Postgres)
 
 Use a single transaction with row locking:
 
 1. Find first eligible task by rank order.
 2. Lock candidate row with `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`.
 3. Update claim fields (`claimed_by_agent_id`, `claimed_at`, `claim_expires_at`, `claim_token`, `state='in_progress'`, `attempt_count=attempt_count+1`).
 4. Commit and return claimed task.
 
 This guarantees agent1 gets task1, agent2 gets task2, etc. without duplicate claims.
 
 ### Fan-out behavior
 
 - Every agent calls `NextTask` asynchronously.
 - If tasks are available, calls distribute work across many agents concurrently.
 
 ### Fan-in behavior
 
 - If no task is currently eligible, return `no_task_available` (not error).
 - Agents backoff/jitter and retry, or wake via event/notification when dependency state changes.
 - Completion of prerequisite tasks naturally unlocks dependent tasks for future `NextTask` calls.
 
 ### Lease and recovery rules
 
 - If an agent crashes, expired lease makes task reclaimable.
 - Optional heartbeat may extend `claim_expires_at` for long-running tasks.
 - A completion/failure write must validate `claim_token` so only the current holder can finalize.
 
 ### Ingestion retry continuity (same asynq task invocation)
 
 - Taskboard generation/validation retries occur inside one asynq task invocation (no handoff to a new worker task for each validation retry).
 - Retry attempts must reuse and carry forward available run continuity metadata (`agent_session_id`, `agent_stream_id`).
 - Persist the latest known continuity metadata on the task model audit so resume/follow-up calls can continue the same agent stream/session when supported.
 
 ## State Transition Guidance
 
 - board_state: pending -> active -> completed | failed
 - epic_state: planned -> in_progress -> completed | blocked | failed
 - task_state: planned -> in_progress -> completed | failed | no_work_needed
 - outcome_status: success | partial | failed
 
 ## Implementation Notes
 
 - Keep orchestration in application layer.
 - Keep invariants and transitions in domain layer.
 - Keep Postgres-specific details in infrastructure repositories.
 - Keep API/worker correlation identifiers persisted via model_run_id and upstream run/task/job IDs.
 
 ## Scope Guardrails (Explicit)
 
 - Ignore redesign of already-implemented agent task execution internals.
 - Existing agent task execution code should only be touched enough to compile against this new contract.
 - Primary focus is wiring, the new tracker domain contract, and the atomic fan-in/fan-out `NextTask` scheduler.
 - Do not introduce additional Postgres tables for this tracker rewrite unless absolutely necessary.
 - Prefer extending the three existing tracker tables and their columns/indexes over adding new tables.
 
 ## Go Domain Struct Model
 
 ```go
 package domain
 
 import (
  "time"
 
  "github.com/google/uuid"
 )
 
 type BoardState string
 
 const (
  BoardStatePending   BoardState = "pending"
  BoardStateActive    BoardState = "active"
  BoardStateCompleted BoardState = "completed"
  BoardStateFailed    BoardState = "failed"
 )
 
 type EpicState string
 
 const (
  EpicStatePlanned    EpicState = "planned"
  EpicStateInProgress EpicState = "in_progress"
  EpicStateCompleted  EpicState = "completed"
  EpicStateBlocked    EpicState = "blocked"
  EpicStateFailed     EpicState = "failed"
 )
 
 type TaskState string
 
 const (
  TaskStatePlanned    TaskState = "planned"
  TaskStateInProgress TaskState = "in_progress"
  TaskStateCompleted  TaskState = "completed"
  TaskStateFailed     TaskState = "failed"
  TaskStateNoWorkNeeded TaskState = "no_work_needed"
 )
 
 type OutcomeStatus string
 
 const (
  OutcomeStatusSuccess OutcomeStatus = "success"
  OutcomeStatusPartial OutcomeStatus = "partial"
  OutcomeStatusFailed  OutcomeStatus = "failed"
 )
 
 type ProjectBoard struct {
  ID        uuid.UUID
  ProjectID uuid.UUID
  Name      string
  State     BoardState
  CreatedAt time.Time
  UpdatedAt time.Time
 }
 
 type ProjectBoardEpic struct {
  ID        uuid.UUID
  BoardID   uuid.UUID
  Title     string
  Objective *string
  State     EpicState
  Rank      int
  DependsOnEpicIDs []uuid.UUID
  CreatedAt time.Time
  UpdatedAt time.Time
 }
 
 type TaskModelAudit struct {
  ModelProvider     string
  ModelName         string
  ModelVersion      *string
  ModelRunID        *uuid.UUID
 	AgentSessionID    *string
 	AgentStreamID     *string
  PromptFingerprint *string
  InputTokens       *int
  OutputTokens      *int
  StartedAt         *time.Time
  CompletedAt       *time.Time
 }
 
 type TaskOutcome struct {
  Status       OutcomeStatus
  Summary      string
  ErrorCode    *string
  ErrorMessage *string
 }
 
 type ProjectBoardTask struct {
  ID        uuid.UUID
  BoardID   uuid.UUID
  EpicID    uuid.UUID
  Title     string
  TaskType  string
  State     TaskState
  Rank      int
  DependsOnTaskIDs []uuid.UUID
  Audit     TaskModelAudit
  Outcome   TaskOutcome
  CreatedAt time.Time
  UpdatedAt time.Time
 }
 ```
 
 ### Validation expectations for the Go model
 
 - `ProjectBoardTask.BoardID` must match the owning epic `BoardID`.
 - `ProjectBoardEpic.DependsOnEpicIDs` can only contain epics from the same board and cannot include itself.
 - `ProjectBoardTask.DependsOnTaskIDs` can only contain tasks from the same board and cannot include itself.
 - `TaskOutcome.Status` is always required.
 - `TaskOutcome.Summary` is the only outcome content field (no large payload/blob field).
 - `TaskModelAudit.ModelProvider` and `TaskModelAudit.ModelName` are required for model-produced results.
 - `TaskModelAudit.AgentSessionID` and `TaskModelAudit.AgentStreamID` are optional but should be set when the agent runtime provides resumable session/stream identifiers.
 - If `TaskOutcome.Status == OutcomeStatusFailed`, `TaskOutcome.ErrorCode` must be present.

1. # V1 Placement Rules
2. 
3. ## Purpose
4. 
5. Define canonical package placement and dependency direction for all V1 implementation work.
6. 
7. ## Canonical Structure
8. 
9. V1 is layer-first with slot boundaries mirrored inside layers.
10. 
11. - `internal/domain/<slot>`
12. - `internal/application/<slot>`
13. - `internal/infrastructure/<slot>`
14. - `internal/interface/*` (delivery surfaces only)
15. - `cmd/*` (composition roots only)
16. 
17. Canonical slots: `agent`, `scm`, `tracker`, `notifier`, `client`.
18. 
19. ## Mandatory Dependency Direction
20. 
21. - `interface -> application -> domain`
22. - `infrastructure` implements ports/contracts owned by inner layers
23. - `domain` must not depend on `application`, `interface`, or `infrastructure`
24. 
25. ## Layer Ownership
26. 
27. - `cmd/*`
28.   - Startup wiring and composition roots only.
29.   - No business orchestration.
30. - `internal/interface/*`
31.   - Transport, validation, mapping, and delivery handlers.
32.   - No business rules.
33. - `internal/application/*`
34.   - Use-case orchestration and process boundaries.
35.   - Coordinates domain + ports.
36. - `internal/domain/*`
37.   - Business meaning, invariants, value objects, aggregates, domain services.
38. - `internal/infrastructure/*`
39.   - Concrete adapters for queue, SCM, persistence, APIs, filesystem, runtime technology.
40. 
41. ## Forbidden Placement
42. 
43. - Business logic in `cmd/*` or `internal/interface/*`.
44. - Direct `interface -> domain` bypass for orchestration.
45. - Infrastructure packages owning use-case orchestration.
46. - New slot-first roots (`pkg/agent`, `pkg/scm`, etc.) as primary architecture.
47. 
48. ## Placement Decision Rule
49. 
50. When placement is ambiguous:
51. 
52. 1. Put business meaning/invariants in `domain`.
53. 2. Put orchestration in `application`.
54. 3. Put adapters in `infrastructure`.
55. 4. Keep transport concerns in `interface`.
56. 5. Keep process wiring in `cmd`.
57. 
58. If a requested placement violates these rules, the change must be redirected before implementation.
59.

 # V1 Placement Rules
 
 ## Purpose
 
 Define canonical package placement and dependency direction for all V1 implementation work.
 
 ## Canonical Structure
 
 V1 is layer-first with slot boundaries mirrored inside layers.
 
 - `internal/domain/<slot>`
 - `internal/application/<slot>`
 - `internal/infrastructure/<slot>`
 - `internal/interface/*` (delivery surfaces only)
 - `cmd/*` (composition roots only)
 
 Canonical slots: `agent`, `scm`, `tracker`, `notifier`, `client`.
 
 ## Mandatory Dependency Direction
 
 - `interface -> application -> domain`
 - `infrastructure` implements ports/contracts owned by inner layers
 - `domain` must not depend on `application`, `interface`, or `infrastructure`
 
 ## Layer Ownership
 
 - `cmd/*`
   - Startup wiring and composition roots only.
   - No business orchestration.
 - `internal/interface/*`
   - Transport, validation, mapping, and delivery handlers.
   - No business rules.
 - `internal/application/*`
   - Use-case orchestration and process boundaries.
   - Coordinates domain + ports.
 - `internal/domain/*`
   - Business meaning, invariants, value objects, aggregates, domain services.
 - `internal/infrastructure/*`
   - Concrete adapters for queue, SCM, persistence, APIs, filesystem, runtime technology.
 
 ## Forbidden Placement
 
 - Business logic in `cmd/*` or `internal/interface/*`.
 - Direct `interface -> domain` bypass for orchestration.
 - Infrastructure packages owning use-case orchestration.
 - New slot-first roots (`pkg/agent`, `pkg/scm`, etc.) as primary architecture.
 
 ## Placement Decision Rule
 
 When placement is ambiguous:
 
 1. Put business meaning/invariants in `domain`.
 2. Put orchestration in `application`.
 3. Put adapters in `infrastructure`.
 4. Keep transport concerns in `interface`.
 5. Keep process wiring in `cmd`.
 
 If a requested placement violates these rules, the change must be redirected before implementation.

1. # Slice 01 — SCM Core (GitHub First, Agents Second)
2. 
3. ## Status
4. 
5. - Completed: **Completed**
6. - Reviewed At: **2026-03-01T01:25:40Z**
7. 
8. ## Objective
9. 
10. Deliver SCM and agent orchestration with strict DDD boundaries, then harden reliability with durable Postgres-backed execution state.
11. 
12. ## Delivery Order
13. 
14. 1. **Part 01A — SCM for GitHub (Full Vertical Slice)**
15. 2. **Part 01B — SCM for Agents**
16. 3. **Reliability Hardening (Postgres-backed persistence)**
17. 
18. ## Part 01A — SCM for GitHub (Full Vertical Slice)
19. 
20. ### Scope
21. 
22. Implement a complete GitHub-first SCM vertical slice across API admission, taskengine dispatch, worker execution, and infrastructure adapters:
23. 
24. - Source-state operations
25. - Repository checkout lifecycle (`create`, `sync/update`, `cleanup`)
26. - Branch lifecycle
27. - Pull request lifecycle
28. - Review lifecycle
29. - Merge-intent/merge-readiness operations
30. 
31. ### Task Checklist
32. 
33. - [x] Define `internal/domain/scm` contracts and invariants for source/repository/branch/PR/review/merge-intent.
34. - [x] Implement `internal/application/scm` use-cases for full SCM management flows.
35. - [x] Add SCM API admission contract (GraphQL/control-plane).
36. - [x] Add SCM job kind/policy wiring in taskengine for queue, idempotency, timeout, and retry defaults.
37. - [x] Implement worker SCM handlers that execute SCM flows through application services.
38. - [x] Implement worker-only SCM authentication contract and runtime execution path.
39. - [x] Add first concrete `internal/infrastructure/scm` GitHub adapter.
40. - [x] Add typed failure classification (`transient` vs `terminal`) and retry-safe mapping.
41. - [x] Add checkpoint/resume boundaries for long-running SCM worker operations.
42. - [x] Add integration tests for API admission -> queue -> worker -> SCM adapter path.
43. 
44. ## Part 01B — SCM for Agents
45. 
46. ### Scope
47. 
48. Implement agent-facing SCM orchestration that consumes Part 01A SCM contracts through application ports only.
49. 
50. ### Task Checklist
51. 
52. - [x] Define `internal/domain/agent` contracts referencing SCM capabilities via ports.
53. - [x] Implement `internal/application/agent` orchestration for SCM-driven flows.
54. - [x] Align correlation and idempotency contracts across agent + SCM operations.
55. - [x] Add checkpoint/resume boundaries for long-running agent+SCM orchestration.
56. - [x] Add worker handler integration points for agent tasks invoking SCM application services.
57. - [x] Add integration tests for agent orchestration through SCM ports.
58. 
59. ## Reliability Hardening — Persistence Alignment
60. 
61. ### Scope
62. 
63. Make execution reliability durable by persisting state transitions and resumability signals in Postgres.
64. 
65. ### Task Checklist
66. 
67. - [x] Persist execution state in Postgres (`job_execution_events`) for deterministic replay/audit.
68. - [x] Persist workflow admission writes (`queued`) for `workflow_runs/workflow_tasks/workflow_jobs`.
69. - [x] Persist dead-letter triage requeue audit events (`dead_letter_events`).
70. - [x] Replace in-memory SCM lease manager with Postgres lease manager (`scm_repo_leases`).
71. 
72. ## AO-Informed Runtime Patterns (Adopted)
73. 
74. The `.docs/agent-orchestrator` review reinforced the following patterns, which are now part of Slice 01 operating contracts:
75. 
76. - [x] **Correlation-first tracing**: propagate `run_id/task_id/job_id` across admission, worker execution, and persistence surfaces.
77. - [x] **Checkpoint-guarded idempotent handlers**: load checkpoint, short-circuit completed steps, then persist next checkpoint boundary.
78. - [x] **Typed failure mapping**: classify failures as `transient` vs `terminal` for deterministic retry/escalation behavior.
79. - [x] **Append-only operational audit trail**: persist admission and dead-letter events; persist execution lifecycle records for replay/debug.
80. - [x] **Worker-owned provider execution**: keep SCM auth/provider calls in worker runtime only; interface/application remain orchestration-only.
81. 
82. ## Deliverables
83. 
84. - GitHub-first SCM contracts and adapters for source/repository/branch/PR/review/merge-intent.
85. - Agent orchestration contracts that compose SCM through ports.
86. - Durable reliability foundation in Postgres for retries, execution status, admission, dead-letter auditing, and SCM lease coordination.
87. - AO-aligned runtime execution patterns codified as Slice 01 baseline behavior.
88. 
89. ## In Scope
90. 
91. - Part 01A full GitHub-first SCM vertical slice.
92. - Part 01B agent orchestration consuming SCM application services.
93. - Postgres-backed reliability persistence needed by worker execution and operations.
94. - AO-derived reliability/runtime patterns listed above.
95. 
96. ## Out of Scope
97. 
98. - Multi-provider parity beyond GitHub baseline.
99. - UI/client product flows beyond required control-plane admission.
100. - Non-worker authentication execution paths.

1. # Slice 02 — Worker Execution Plane
2. 
3. ## Status
4. 
5. - Completed: **Completed**
6. - Reviewed At: **2026-03-01T01:25:40Z**
7. 
8. ## Objective
9. 
10. Deliver one execution plane that supports local and remote workers with shared dispatch, lease, and resume semantics, backed by durable Postgres persistence for execution-critical state.
11. 
12. ## Task Checklist
13. 
14. - [x] Introduce queue/task-engine abstraction in application layer.
15. - [x] Add first concrete queue adapter (`asynq`) in infrastructure layer.
16. - [x] Wire worker runtime bootstrap to start queue consumer.
17. - [x] Register worker handlers through the task engine path.
18. - [x] Define worker capability advertisement contract.
19. - [x] Implement remote worker adapter contract and SCM-backed remote bootstrap sequence.
20. - [x] Persist execution state in Postgres (`job_execution_events`).
21. - [x] Replace in-memory SCM lease coordination with Postgres lease manager (`scm_repo_leases`).
22. 
23. ## Deliverables
24. 
25. - Dispatch + lease model with deterministic ownership semantics.
26. - Checkpoint/resume contract with Postgres as durability layer.
27. - Execution journal contract with Postgres as durability layer.
28. - Local/remote execution parity path under shared contracts.
29. 
30. ## In Scope
31. 
32. - Queue/task engine abstraction and concrete adapter wiring.
33. - Worker lifecycle states and typed failure handling.
34. - Correlation IDs across dispatch, execution, and completion.
35. - Postgres persistence for execution-critical operational state.
36. 
37. ## Out of Scope
38. 
39. - Provider-specific optimization beyond baseline reliability.
40. - UI-specific worker fleet controls beyond control-plane APIs.
41. 
42. ## Acceptance Criteria
43. 
44. - Same job contract runs through local and remote execution paths.
45. - Transient failures resume from persisted Postgres checkpoints.
46. - Execution status is recoverable from Postgres after worker restart.
47. 
48. ## Dependencies
49. 
50. - Slice 00 complete.
51. - Slice 01 complete.
52. - Container runtime alignment with slice 08.
53. 
54. ## Exit Check
55. 
56. This slice is complete when execution is location-agnostic and reliability-critical state can be reconstructed from Postgres.
